animals.name: Beecroft's Flying Squirrel
animals.description: A gliding rodent that uses a membrane stretched between limbs to travel silently between trees.

animals.name: Cameroon Scaly-tail
animals.description: Despite superficial similarities to true flying squirrels, this is an independent evolutionary lineage — equally capable.

animals.name: Derby's Scaly-tailed Squirrel
animals.description: This African flying squirrel analog detects threats from the canopy and responds with evasive gliding flight.

animals.name: Dwarf Scaly-tailed Squirrel

animals.name: Long-eared Flying Mouse
animals.description: Its scaly tail grips bark on landing — a controlled, near-silent aerial approach that unnerves prey and predators alike.

animals.name: Pel's Scaly-tailed Flying Squirrel
animals.description: A large gliding rodent of the Central African rainforest, entirely distinct from the flying squirrels of Sciuridae despite superficial resemblance — a classic case of convergent evolution. It uses rows of spiny scales on its tail to grip bark when landing. Mostly nocturnal and poorly studied, which makes it dangerous in ways we do not fully understand.

animals.name: Pygmy Scaly-tailed Flying Squirrel
animals.description: Despite superficial similarities to true flying squirrels, this is an independent evolutionary lineage — equally capable.

animals.name: East African Springhare
animals.description: A bipedal sprinter with a thick balancing tail, capable of sudden direction changes mid-leap.

animals.name: South African Springhare
animals.description: Southern Africa's answer to the kangaroo — a large, powerful jumping rodent that can cover 2 meters per leap and reaches speeds of 45 km/h when fleeing predators. Its powerful hind claws are capable of raking wounds, and it has a thick muscular tail used for balance and sudden redirection. An ambush predator's nightmare.

animals.name: Eurasian Beaver
animals.description: The Eurasian beaver is the slightly larger of the two living beaver species, once hunted to near-extinction across Europe for its fur and castoreum. A tireless builder and a ferocious defender of its territory — river otters and wolves have been driven off by a determined adult beaver protecting a dam.

animals.name: North American Beaver
animals.description: The largest rodent of North America and an ecosystem engineer of rivers. Equipped with ironically iron-reinforced incisors that never stop growing, a flat paddle tail it can use as a weapon, and a second eyelid for underwater vision. Slower than most rodents but armored in thick fur, fearless, and capable of felling trees. In combat, it is a siege engine: difficult to kill, focused, and devastating at close range.

animals.description: An armored tunneling machine. The pocket gopher's skull is massively reinforced for excavation, and its incisors protrude in front of its lips so it can bite and dig simultaneously without swallowing soil. Aggressive in its burrow to a pathological degree — it will attack animals many times its size that invade its tunnel.

animals.name: Banner-tailed Kangaroo Rat
animals.description: Built like a tiny kangaroo, the kangaroo rat is an evasion specialist of the American desert. It can detect rattlesnake strikes by the vibration through the ground and has evolved a high-amplitude leap that carries it clear of most ambushes. Its foot-drumming is a direct signal to would-be predators: I know you are there.

animals.name: Wild Guinea Pig
animals.description: The wild ancestor of the domestic guinea pig, native to the grasslands and rocky areas of South America. More alert, leaner, and faster than its domesticated cousin. Entirely precocial — born with open eyes and full fur — and functionally running within hours of birth. In the arena its stats are unremarkable but its threat perception is not.

animals.name: Chacoan Mara
animals.description: Larger than it appears, with a powerful bite capable of drawing blood from most attackers.

animals.name: Patagonian Mara
animals.description: A large cursorial rodent that fills the ecological niche of a small deer across the Patagonian steppe. It runs like a dog, bounces like a rabbit, and sprints like a gazelle — up to 45 km/h. Monogamous and highly attentive to offspring, it uses its long legs to deliver powerful donkey-kicks when cornered.

animals.name: Capybara
animals.description: The world's largest living rodent. Semi-aquatic, highly social, and alarmingly calm about most threats — including apex predators. A capybara can hold its breath for five minutes, run at 35 km/h in short bursts, and bite through a concrete block with its chisel incisors. Its true strength is social: groups function as a collective security apparatus.

animals.name: Lesser Capybara
animals.description: This rodent's communal structure makes individual predation extremely difficult — the herd absorbs most threats.

animals.name: Ecuadorean Viscacha
animals.description: Highly agile in rocky terrain, using small ledges and crevices that no predator can safely navigate.

animals.name: Long-tailed Chinchilla
animals.description: Native to the Andes at elevations above 4,000 meters, the chinchilla's fur is the densest of any land mammal — 60 hairs per follicle, arranged to shed heat and moisture in an instant. It can survive temperatures that would kill most rodents. When threatened it releases chunks of its own fur (fur-slip) to escape a predator's grip. It is also a surprisingly powerful biter for its size.

animals.name: Northern Viscacha
animals.description: A social species whose alert network makes individual surprise attacks virtually impossible in its rocky terrain.

animals.name: Plains Viscacha
animals.description: A stocky, heavy-set rodent of the Argentine pampas with one of the highest brain-to-body ratios among rodents. Viscacha colonies construct elaborate burrow networks called viscacheras, which become long-term landmarks on the landscape used by generations. The male plains viscacha is significantly larger than the female and highly territorial.

animals.name: Short-tailed Chinchilla
animals.description: Its soft, hyper-dense fur paradoxically helps shed grasping claws — predators lose their grip at the last moment.

animals.name: Southern Viscacha
animals.description: One of the fastest small rodents in its Andean habitat — capable of bounding between rocks at startling speed.

The pacarana is slow, rotund, and rarely seen, haunting Andean cloud forests in small family groups. What it lacks in speed it compensates with mass, powerful incisors, and the eerie composure of an animal that has almost no natural fear.

animals.name: North American Porcupine
animals.description: The arboreal counterpart to the Old World porcupine. North America's only quilled mammal carries up to 30,000 barbed quills — retrobarbed so that muscle movement drives them deeper with each passing minute. It is slow, confident, and almost completely fearless. There is no reason to hurry when you are the most uncomfortable thing in the forest to bite.

animals.name: Paraguayan Hairy Dwarf Porcupine
animals.description: Predators that learn the hard way: this porcupine's quills detach on contact and work their way deeper.

animals.name: Prehensile-tailed Porcupine
animals.description: An armored rodent that faces threats with casual indifference, relying on passive defenses others envy.

animals.name: Roosmalen's Dwarf Porcupine
animals.description: A slow-moving but nearly untouchable creature whose 30,000 barbed quills make every attack a costly mistake.

animals.name: Rothschild's Porcupine
animals.description: Deceptively dangerous — its shuffle suggests weakness, but a tail swipe can embed dozens of quills.

animals.name: Stump-tailed Porcupine
animals.description: Predators that learn the hard way: this porcupine's quills detach on contact and work their way deeper.

animals.name: Naked Mole-rat
animals.description: One of the most biologically anomalous mammals alive. The naked mole-rat is eusocial like a bee, nearly insensitive to acid-burn pain, resistant to cancer, capable of surviving 18 minutes without oxygen by switching to fructose metabolism, and lives up to 32 years — ten times the expected lifespan for its size. It does not feel the bite of an enemy the way other animals do. It just keeps fighting.

animals.name: African Brush-tailed Porcupine
animals.description: The Old World's answer to armored defense — this porcupine rattles its quills as a warning before unleashing them.

animals.name: Crested Porcupine
animals.description: The largest rodent in Africa and one of the most effectively armored animals of its size. Its quills are not passive — the porcupine charges backwards into threats, driving 35-cm hollow quills deep into flesh with enough force to kill lions. Documented kills include leopards, hyenas, and large pythons. No one rushes a porcupine twice.

animals.name: Cape Porcupine

animals.name: Degu
animals.description: A diurnal social rodent of the Chilean pre-Andes, remarkable for its intelligence and complex social structures. Degus are used as models in neuroscience research because of their unusual sugar metabolism and social cognition. Like chinchillas, they can shed fur when seized. Their alarm calls encode specific threat information and are transmitted socially.

animals.name: Golden Hamster
animals.description: In the wild, the golden hamster is a solitary, ferociously territorial animal found in the arid highlands of Syria. It digs complex burrow systems and will fight other hamsters to the death over territory. Its reputation as a gentle pet is an artifact of millennia of captive breeding — in its ancestral form, it is a pugnacious and stubborn fighter.

animals.name: Northern Grasshopper Mouse
animals.description: The only truly carnivorous mouse in North America. It howls. It hunts scorpions — and has evolved a neurological adaptation that reroutes scorpion venom as a painkiller rather than suffering from it. The grasshopper mouse approaches combat the way a wolf does: methodically, fearlessly, going for the head.

animals.name: House Mouse
animals.description: The archetype of small and quick. Few animals have been as thoroughly studied, but in the wild the house mouse is an adaptable, cunning survivor that has spread to every corner of the Earth. It is outclassed by almost everything in a straight fight — but it is faster, sneakier, and harder to catch than most give it credit for.

animals.name: African Crested Rat
animals.description: The only mammal known to sequester plant toxins into its fur as a defensive chemical weapon. The African crested rat chews bark of the Acokanthera tree — source of ouabain, an arrow poison — and coats specialized absorbent hairs along its flank. A single lick from a dog has caused death. When threatened it erects its crest and parts its fur to expose the toxic hairs, daring anything to touch it.
